levels.body: The name for the female protagonist of the poem derives from Greek *porphyros*, meaning *purple*.
worksCited: "purple, *adj*. and *n*." *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, June 2022, www.oed.com/view/Entry/154956. Accessed 14 July 2022.

levels.body: The word “porphyria” is understood either as the royal color in Roman times or a symbol of the deepest lust (Gage 25). According to Oxford English Dictionary, when used in poetic works the word “purple” is associated with bloodiness and bloodstain (“purple,* adj.*” 1.I.2.).
worksCited: Gage, John. *Color and Culture: Practice and Meaning from Antiquity to Abstraction*. University of California Press, 1999. *Google Books*. books.google.pl/books?id=oq_GtjmoTNgC&printsec=copyright&hl=pl#v=one page&q&f=false  Accessed 6 April 2023.
"purple, *adj*. and *n*." *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, June 2022, www.oed.com/view/Entry/154956. Accessed 14 July 2022.

levels.body: The name “Porphyria,” on the one hand, is a means of characterization of the woman and of her relationship to the speaker. The royal or imperial connotation of the word suggests porphyria’s nobility, and the connotation of “deepest lust” can be found in the erotic description of her in line 17. On the other hand, the name foreshadows the association of the “bloodiness” that is implicitly linked to the crime that will later be carried out by the speaker against Porphyria.
